text: The surgeon begins dissecting the mid portion of the surgical field, focusing on the area surrounding the hernia sac.
The surgical field shows a large peritoneal gap with no visible hernia sac present for dissection.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the ipsilateral posterior rectal sheath and is now entering the contralateral retroactive space, preparing for further tissue manipulation.
The video shows the completed dissection in the retroactive space, with the anatomical structures clearly separated and exposed.
The surgeon begins cutting through the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, starting in the middle of the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon examines the dissection site, realizing that only the posterior lamella of the internal oblique has been cut, rather than both layers as initially thought.
The surgeon uses scissors to shimmy and find the correct plane after cutting through one layer of tissue.
The surgeon completes the incision through both layers of the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis, preparing to further dissect the transversus abdominis muscle.
The surgeon cuts through the posterior lamella of the anterior oblique and the transverse abdominus, completing the transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.
The surgeon performs a transverse abdominis release (TAR) to detach the posterior rectus sheath from its attachments at the linea semilunaris, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon medializes the posterior rectus sheath to close the central gap, facilitating flap closure without significant tension.
The surgeon is in the preperitoneal space, carefully separating the peritoneum from the underlying transverse salus fascia using surgical instruments.
The surgeon navigates towards the pre-transverse salus fascia plane in the upper abdomen, preparing to enter this anatomical space.
The surgeon proceeds cephalad to further release the posterior rectus sheath from its attachments, continuing the dissection in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon enters the pre-transverse salus fascia plane, continuing the dissection within the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon navigates the space between the transverse salus fascia and the transverse abdominis, revealing the anatomical separation between these layers.
The surgeon continues releasing tissue in the pre-transverse salus fascia plane to facilitate closure of the central defect, aiming to minimize tension during the closure process.
The surgical field shows the final result with the central defect closed, displaying the completed closure without visible tension.
